title: Refund-Recharge Cycling to Artificially Inflate Transaction Volume and Revenue Recognition
narrative: Fraudster exploits merchant refund processing to create artificial transaction volume. Pattern begins with 8-12 legitimate-appearing purchases ($45-$85 each, ProductCD='W' for digital goods) from same card within 90 minutes. Merchant processes refunds within 2-4 hours. Immediately after each refund posts, fraudster initiates new purchase at slightly higher amount ($50-$95), creating upward spiral. Cycle repeats 3-5 times over 18-36 hours. Each iteration shows card1_txn_count_1hr jumping from baseline 2 to 6-9, then resetting post-refund. TransactionAmt creeps upward 8-15% per cycle. Fraudster uses consistent P_emaildomain (often gmail.com) but rotates addr1 values across 3-4 ZIP codes. Final cycle typically involves bust-out: $400-$800 purchase with no subsequent refund request. Refund requests show identical R_emaildomain to purchase emails, suggesting single-actor control of both sides.
indicators: 3-5 complete purchase-refund-repurchase cycles within 36 hours on single card
TransactionAmt increases 8-15% with each cycle iteration
card1_txn_count_1hr spikes to 6-9 then drops to 0-1 post-refund, repeating pattern
Refund processing time consistently 2-4 hours, new purchase within 15 minutes of refund posting
P_emaildomain matches R_emaildomain across all transactions (single-party control)
Final transaction in sequence 4-9x larger than cycle average with no refund request
addr1 rotates across 3-4 different ZIP codes while maintaining same card and email
summary: A single card makes 8-12 small purchases, requests refunds within hours, then immediately repurchases at slightly higher amounts—repeating this cycle 3-5 times over 36 hours with transaction amounts climbing 8-15% each round, culminating in one large final purchase with no refund request.
reasoning: The fraudster manipulates refund processing to artificially inflate transaction volume and test merchant controls, using each refund to reset velocity limits before pushing slightly higher amounts, ultimately executing a bust-out purchase once confidence in the merchant's lax oversight is established.
indicator_explanations: Card cycles through buy-refund-rebuy loop multiple times in rapid succession
Each new purchase amount creeps higher than the previous cycle
Transaction count surges then flatlines after refunds in repeating waves
Refunds process quickly and new purchases fire immediately after posting
Same email controls both purchase and refund sides of transactions
Final purchase is dramatically larger with no subsequent refund requested
Billing address changes repeatedly while card and email stay constant
